name: Remo Palmier
abstract: Remo Paul Palmier (March 29, 1923 – February 2, 2002) was an American jazz guitarist.

about_quotes.speaker: Jay Leonhart
about_quotes.evidence: Remo Palmieri is a wonderful guitar player who now still lives in New York who I’ve since worked with a number of times, we’re very good friends.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jay Leonhart interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 15, 1998 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jay Leonhart
about_quotes.evidence: Remo Palmieri is a wonderful guitar player who now still lives in New York who I’ve since worked with a number of times, we’re very good friends.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jay Leonhart interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Hartford, New York, September 27, 2013 [interview 2 of 2] [transcript only]

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: Red Norvo had an orchestra composed of Joe (Flip) Phillips, Shorty Rodgers, Aaron Sachs, Eddie Bert, Remo Palmieri, Teddy Wilson and Slam Stewart.

about_quotes.speaker: John Levy
about_quotes.evidence: The Bobby Tucker Quintet consisted of Denzil along with Mundell Lowe on guitar (Remo couldn't make it), Tony Scott on clarinet, Bobby and me.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Levy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Vincent Pelote
about_quotes.evidence: In fact I had a guitar teacher, Remo Palmier, who said to me, “I don’t do that four to the bar nonsense.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Vincent Pelote interviewed by Monk Rowe, Orlando, Florida, February 25, 2017

about_quotes.speaker: Red Norvo
about_quotes.evidence: I had a little group at the Hickory House -- with Aaron and, you know, some more people -- Remo Palmieri
